title: Is One Day Enough to Recount the Miseries of the “Amma” Regime?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil text below is a reading copy of the preserved OCR transcription. The source-confirmed OCR error `மூளைச் Feveneu (Brain-Wash)` has been corrected here to the printed reading `மூளைச் சலவை (Brain-Wash)`. The canonical Tamil chapter in its original folder remains unchanged. No other suspicious form is corrected merely from dictionary or model consensus. Is one day enough to recount them?
paragraphs: Official announcements state that the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly will meet on the 23rd, that the session will last a week, and that the First Supplementary Statement of Expenditure for 2013-14 will be placed before the House on 25 October 2013 under Rule 189(1) of the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly Rules. Though the Assembly is to sit for only a few days, the newspapers have proclaimed their expectation that several important decisions may be taken.

We have spoken firmly, through the TESO movement, to exert the strongest pressure for India to send no representative to the Commonwealth Conference in Sri Lanka: opposing the Sinhala government's continuing and utterly inhuman attacks upon Sri Lankan Tamils; pointing to the Sri Lankan government's war crimes and human-rights violations; and condemning the genocidal slaughter of hundreds of thousands of Tamils in 2009. Like us, several other important parties and student movements in Tamil Nadu have taken the same position. In particular, Viduthalai Chiruthaigal Katchi leader Thol. Thirumavalavan on 15 October 2013, PMK founder Dr Ramadoss on 16 October 2013, and Dravidar Kazhagam president K. Veeramani have all issued statements insisting that India must not participate in the conference and must boycott it. On behalf of the Tamil Nadu government, the Chief Minister too conveyed the same request by letter to the Prime Minister on 18 October 2013. Even earlier, she had urged the Prime Minister to boycott the Commonwealth Conference scheduled to be held in Colombo. Canada's announcement that it would boycott the conference, and the condemnation expressed by Members of Parliament in Britain over its being held in Sri Lanka, are noteworthy turns of events.

When Comrade Thiagu undertook a fast unto death with this same demand, Prime Minister Dr Manmohan Singh wrote to me: “I wish to inform you that a decision on the issue of my participation in the CHOGM Conference will be taken only after considering all relevant factors, including the sentiments of your Party and the Tamil People. I therefore request you to use your good offices to persuade Thiru Thiagu to call off his fast.”

In this situation, it would be fitting for the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly unanimously to adopt and send a resolution to the Union Government. It would demonstrate once again that the feelings of the whole of Tamil Nadu are united on this issue. It would also show the international community that, although Tamil Nadu's parties may hold different views on other questions, all of us stand united in the effort to protect Eelam Tamils. I therefore urge the ruling party to come forward and have such a resolution passed in the Assembly.

Once the Governor has fixed a date for the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly to meet and the news has appeared in the press, it is neither customary nor consistent with established convention for the Government to issue major announcements. Doing so adds no dignity to the Assembly. Yet although the Government announced on the 14th that the House would meet on the 23rd, a succession of announcements has since been issued in the name of Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's orders: Rs. 4.5 crore to improve the Nungambakkam Tennis Stadium; Rs. 1 crore for the Egmore Museum; a prehistoric park at Poondi for Rs. 20 lakh; Rs. 180 crore for sixteen railway overbridges in ten districts; village poverty-eradication societies in fifteen districts at a cost of Rs. 232.30 crore; Rs. 11 crore to renovate the National Art Gallery in Chennai; new concessions for Forest Department employees; Rs. 717 crore for combined drinking-water schemes; new hostel buildings for Anna University's regional offices; and, even today, ten new government hostels for working women at a cost of Rs. 24 crore, Rs. 287 crore to improve irrigation facilities, a new satellite town with 19,500 plots, and Rs. 379 crore for 1,500 homes at Sholinganallur. All these announcements violate convention. If the Government were to say, “This is not the first time we have done this; even after announcing that the Budget would be presented, we issued many such announcements,” it would merely be an exercise in darkness: pointing to one wrong in order to justify another. How many more announcements are still waiting to be read under Rule 110 in the Assembly?

Next, whenever the AIADMK government has a new announcement to make, no minister appears to have permission to announce it. Even if it concerns no more than one crore rupees, it is issued only in the Chief Minister's name. If the Assembly is in session, the Chief Minister reads it in the House under Rule 110. Once a statement is read under that rule, the opposition parties cannot ask any questions about it. They are, however, permitted to praise and thank the Chief Minister who made the announcement!

When the Assembly is not in session, every announcement is released as a government press note in the name of the Chief Minister's orders. Some of these concern allocations for announcements already read by the Chief Minister in the Assembly and praised by the ruling party's allies. Once the Chief Minister signs the allocation files, the fact that she had previously announced the very same schemes is concealed, and they are released as though they were new. Repeatedly issuing hollow announcements for the same scheme may bring publicity, but it will bring no benefit to the people of the State!

For example, on 15 October 2013 the press was told that the Chief Minister had approved sixteen road overbridges at a cost of Rs. 180.89 crore. Yet in a statement read under Rule 110 on 7 September 2011, she had announced that twenty-three new railway overbridges and underpasses, valued at Rs. 740 crore, would be constructed in nine districts. I had even asked: what became of those bridges? Were they completed? There is more amusement still! On 1 April 2013, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa read another statement under Rule 110. She declared with pleasure that eighteen railway overbridges, valued at Rs. 948.30 crore, would be built across eleven districts, and that preliminary work would be undertaken at a cost of Rs. 201.88 crore. She received the praise of those in her ranks as well.

Nearly six months later, the Chief Minister merely granted administrative approval for the overbridges already named in her Rule 110 statement of 1 April 2013. Yet that approval was dressed up as a new announcement, and every newspaper published it under the heading “Chief Minister's Order.” Can those who care for and believe in public welfare and democracy ever accept a government behaving in this fashion, treating people as know-nothings who have lost their memory, subjecting them to a mental “wash” (“Brain-Wash”), and deceiving them? Never, never!

It was the same on 4 April 2013. Under Rule 110, the Chief Minister announced that 3,500 apartment homes would be demolished and replaced by new apartments at a cost of Rs. 280 crore. Administrative approval for that very announcement was granted last September. Once again it was presented as though it were new, and the newspapers carried it prominently as a Chief Minister's order.

In the newspapers dated 21 October 2013, a grand announcement appeared under the headline “Jayalalithaa's Order”: a fully equipped satellite town spread over 587 acres in Madurai. Likewise, this morning came the news that “Chief Minister Amma” had ordered the allocation of Rs. 379.51 crore to construct 1,500 multistorey apartments at Sholinganallur. But six months earlier, on 4 April 2013, the Chief Minister had already declared with pleasure in the Legislative Assembly that a satellite town would be created on 587 acres in Madurai and that 1,500 multistorey apartments would be built at Sholinganallur at an estimated cost of Rs. 612 crore. Six months later, with the Assembly due to meet the next day and perhaps fearing that some DMK MLA might ask, “Where are those schemes?”, they hurriedly granted administrative approval today and presented it in the newspapers like a fresh announcement. When the DMK administration attempted to establish a satellite town at Thirumazhisai, this same Jayalalithaa issued statement after statement opposing it. She would do well to look back at them now. The Government may explain all this during the present session.

On 8 August 2013, newspapers reported that Chief Minister Jayalalithaa had increased to Rs. 50,000 the Family Security Fund paid to the heirs of deceased pensioners. But do you know the truth? The Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly had met in April and May, before this announcement. In the policy note itself, in two places, on pages 2 and 18, what had the Government said? “The Government has now decided to increase from Rs. 35,000 to Rs. 50,000 the financial assistance given under this scheme to families grieving the loss of pensioners.” It had thus been announced in May itself, and the Finance Department had already issued the necessary order. I wrote about this confusion on 11 August 2013. To this day, the Government has not opened its mouth in response.

Next, confusing reports continue to emerge about the arrest of extremists such as Police Fakhruddin, the charges against them, and the rewards given to the police personnel who captured them. What the police now say about the murders to which these men allegedly confessed contradicts earlier statements by the Director General of Police attributing the same crimes to others. If the facts are explained fully in the Assembly, the public will gain clarity. Otherwise, it will become evident that the Government continues to engage in diversionary tactics!

A Union Minister has accused the Tamil Nadu government of failing properly to implement the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme and of paying wages in the names of many who performed no work. So far, the Government has offered no explanation for this serious accusation. At least during this session, it may provide one.

Similarly, a Union Minister has said that delays by the Tamil Nadu government have stalled several Union highway projects worth Rs. 15,000 crore, and that he wishes to seek an appointment with the Chief Minister to discuss the matter. Reports say he also wants to discuss the Maduravoyal-Port elevated expressway. The State Government can tell the Assembly its position. If it does not, the general allegation that the State Government is unwilling to implement Union Government projects will stand proved!

The prices of sand, cement and other materials required for construction have risen under this regime. When cement prices rose during the DMK administration, today's Chief Minister issued a statement condemning it. We may now expect her at least to explain in the Assembly why prices have climbed so high under her own rule.

Then there is law and order, which has become a laughing-stock from town to town under this regime. Several political workers, including members of the BJP, have been murdered. Reports of murder and robbery pile up every day. A bomb reportedly exploded on the grounds of the Police Training College in Coimbatore, seriously injuring seven sub-inspectors who are now receiving hospital treatment. That explosion itself demonstrates the degree of vigilance maintained within the police department under this Government.

As for illegal sand mining, one need hardly ask. A High Court judge has himself condemned it. Matters have reached the point where a District Collector has been suspended. Newspapers have published reports, with photographs, of sand being plundered. As for the plunder of beach minerals, the less said the better. We hear only of inspections and inquiries by officials. So far, there has been no news of meaningful action. A White Paper on the inquiry conducted thus far may be placed before the Assembly.

Newspapers, including The Hindu (Tamil), have reported that Housing Board plots were allotted after understating their market value, that bargaining took place within the department, and that video evidence exists. One person named in that newspaper report, an engineer not even fifty years old, took his own life after seeing the report and died yesterday. I trust that an explanation will be given in the Assembly.

The Chief Minister herself said that Tamil Nadu would lose several hundred crores of rupees because of the Food Security Act enacted by the Union Government. But a Union Minister has said that Tamil Nadu will receive an additional Rs. 600 crore. The Government must explain this in the Assembly as well.

Complaints about hospitals in Tamil Nadu continue to arrive. Reports say that government hospitals in cities such as Madurai, Tiruchi, Salem, Tiruvottiyur and Nagapattinam are not functioning properly. One does not even know whether there is a minister for that department.
As for the farmers' problems, the less said the better.

Having entirely lost the kuruvai crop, farmers were hoping that they might at least obtain something from the samba season. Instead, water has reportedly failed even to reach the tail-end areas, driving them to conduct various protests. It is also said that relief provided by the Government has not properly reached the farmers. Like “letting go of the body and trying to catch the tail,” the Chief Minister has today announced an allocation of Rs. 287 crore to improve irrigation facilities after the cultivation season has ended. Farmers continue to cry out that the procurement prices for paddy and sugarcane are inadequate. The Government must explain this during the session too.

One can speak of the Education Department only with anguish. The fact that four or five people have already been appointed as ministers for this department during the AIADMK's two and a half years in office is enough to understand how it functions. The High Court's observations about the Teachers Recruitment Board are themselves fitting testimony to the standard of this regime. The Government announced the cancellation of tuition fees, but the colleges concerned have still not received the Government's funds.

An announcement about a pay increase for government employees was issued on 26 July 2013. Yet it created a situation in which many employees would receive less than they had previously been drawing. I wrote a letter explaining this at the time. The Government gave no reply. Perhaps it intended to explain itself in the Assembly. Let us hope it will do so now.

Many days have passed since I wrote in detail about the extent to which this regime has channelled advertisements to newspapers favoured by those in power. There has been no reply from the Government. I expect a White Paper to be placed before the Assembly, stating how much has been paid in advertising charges to each newspaper during these two and a half years.

The cruelty of attacks upon Tamil Nadu's fishermen by the Sri Lankan Navy has not ceased. Though every party has raised its voice on behalf of Tamil Nadu's people and fishermen, the cruelty continues. We have appealed to the Union Government countless times. Even yesterday, Rameswaram fishermen were attacked again. The Assembly must adopt a resolution calling upon the Indian Navy henceforth to come to the aid of Indian fishermen and protect them.

The AIADMK government has shown no concern over bonus negotiations or wage increases for transport-corporation workers. Reports say that cloth produced by power-loom workers is lying unsold. Let us hope that, in the Assembly meeting tomorrow, the Government will provide proper answers to all these questions. If I must go on and on recounting the miseries of the AIADMK regime, there will not be enough space; nor will one day be enough!
With affection,
M. K. 22 October 2013
